/wiki/File:76MelbCup.jpgThe 1976 cup won by [Van der Hum](/wiki/Van_der_Hum)
The winner of the first Melbourne Cup in 1861 received a gold watch. The first Melbourne Cup trophy was awarded in 1865 and was an elaborate silver bowl on a stand that had been manufactured in England.[[17]](#cite_note-nma-17) This is currently owned by Australian wine personality [Wolf Blass](/wiki/Wolf_Blass) and is on display at the Wolf Blass Gallery & Museum/Adelaide Hills Wine Bar in Hahndorf, South Australia. The first existing and un-altered Melbourne Cup is from 1866, presented to the owners of [The Barb](/wiki/The_Barb); as of 2013, it is in the [National Museum of Australia](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Australia).[[18]](#cite_note-nma1866-18) The silver trophy presented in 1867, now also in the National Museum of Australia,[[18]](#cite_note-nma1866-18) was also made in England, but jewellers in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia)) complained to the Victorian Racing Club that the trophy should have been made locally. They believed the work of a Melburnian, William Edwards, to be superior in both design and workmanship to the English-made trophy. No trophy was awarded to the Melbourne Cup winner for the next eight years.[[17]](#cite_note-nma-17)

In 1876, [Edward Fischer](/wiki/Edward_Fischer), an immigrant from Austria, produced the first Australian-made trophy. It was an Etruscan shape with two handles. One side depicted a horse race with the grandstand and hill of Flemington in the background. The opposite side had the words "Melbourne Cup, 1876" and the name of the winning horse.[[19]](#cite_note-loving_cup-19) A silver-plated base sporting three silver horses was added in 1888, but in 1891 the prize changed to being a 15 in-high (380 mm), 24 in-long (610 mm) trophy showing a Victory figure offering an olive wreath to a jockey.[[19]](#cite_note-loving_cup-19) From 1899, the trophy was in the form of silver galloping horse embossed on a 3 ft-long (0.9 m) plaque, although it was said to look like a [greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound) by some people.

The last Melbourne Cup trophy manufactured in England was made for the 1914 event. It was a chalice centred on a long base which had a horse at each end. The trophy awarded in 1916,[[20]](#cite_note-20) the first gold trophy, was a three-legged, three-armed rose bowl. The three-handled [loving cup](/wiki/Loving_cup) design was first awarded in 1919. In that year, the Victorian Racing Club had commissioned James Steeth to design a trophy that would be in keeping with the prestige of the race, little realising that it would become the iconic Melbourne Cup still presented today.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the Second World War years (1942, 1943 and 1944), the winning owner received [war bonds](/wiki/War_bond) valued at 200 pounds.

A new trophy is struck each year and becomes the property of the winning owner. In the event of a dead heat, a second cup is on hand. The present trophy is hand spun from 1.65 kg of 18-carat gold.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The winning trainer and jockey also receive a miniature replica of the cup (since 1973) and the [strapper](/wiki/Strapper) is awarded the [Tommy Woodcock](/wiki/Tommy_Woodcock) Trophy, named after the strapper of [Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap).

In 2003, an annual tour of the Melbourne Cup trophy was initiated to provide communities across Australia and New Zealand with an opportunity to view the Cup trophy and highlight the contribution the Melbourne Cup has made to Australia's social, sporting and racing culture. Each year, communities in Australia and New Zealand apply for the cup to tour their community and the tour also takes in cities around the world as part of the Victoria Racing Club's strategy to promote the Melbourne Cup and the Melbourne Cup Carnival internationally.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The Tour has visited schools and aged-care and hospital facilities, and participated in community events and celebrations including race days across Australia and New Zealand.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

/wiki/File:Melbourne_cup_1881.jpgMelbourne Cup in 1881, with engraving on the finish line

[Frederick Standish](/wiki/Frederick_Standish), member of the Victorian Turf Club and steward on the day of the first Cup,[[25]](#cite_note-essay-25) was credited with forming the idea to hold a horse race and calling it the "Melbourne Cup".[[25]](#cite_note-essay-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Seventeen horses contested the first Melbourne Cup on Thursday 7 November 1861, racing for the modest prize of 710 [gold sovereigns](/wiki/Coins_of_Australia) ([£](/wiki/Pound_(currency))710) cash and a hand-beaten gold [watch](/wiki/Watch), winner takes all.[[27]](#cite_note-masters-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The prize was not, as some have suggested, the largest purse up to that time. A large crowd of 4,000 men and women watched the race, although it has been suggested this was less than expected because of news reaching Melbourne of the death of explorers [Burke and Wills](/wiki/Burke_and_Wills)[[25]](#cite_note-essay-25) five days earlier on 2 November. Nevertheless, the attendance was the largest at Flemington on any day for the past two years, with the exception of the recently run Two Thousand Guinea Stakes.[[27]](#cite_note-masters-27)

The winner of this first Melbourne Cup race was a 16.3 [hand](/wiki/Hand_(length)) [bay](/wiki/Bay_(horse)) [stallion](/wiki/Stallion) by the name of [Archer](/wiki/Archer_(horse)) in a time of 3.52.00, ridden by [John Cutts](/wiki/John_Cutts_(jockey)), trained by [Etienne de Mestre](/wiki/Etienne_L._de_Mestre), and leased (and consequently raced in his own name) by de Mestre. As a lessee de Mestre owned and was fully responsible for Archer during the lease. Archer was leased from the "Exeter Farm" of [Jembaicumbene](/wiki/Jembaicumbene) near [Braidwood, New South Wales](/wiki/Braidwood,_New_South_Wales). His owners were Thomas John "Tom" Roberts (a good school-friend of de Mestre's), Rowland H. Hassall (Roberts' brother-in-law), and Edmund Molyneux Royds and William Edward Royds (Roberts' nephews).[[29]](#cite_note-heritage-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Hall-30)[[31]](#cite_note-book-31)

The inaugural Melbourne Cup of 1861 was an eventful affair when one horse bolted before the start, and three of the seventeen starters fell during the race, two of which died. Archer, a Sydney outsider who drew scant favour in the betting, spread-eagled the field and defeated the favourite, and Victorian champion, Mormon by six lengths. Dismissed by the bookies, Archer took a lot of money away from Melbourne, 'refuelling interstate rivalry' and adding to the excitement of the Cup.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The next day, Archer was raced in and won another 2-mile long-distance race, the Melbourne Town Plate.

It has become legend that Archer walked over 800 km (over 500 miles) to Flemington from de Mestre's stable at "Terara" near [Nowra, New South Wales](/wiki/Nowra,_New_South_Wales). However, newspaper archives of the day reveal that he had travelled south from Sydney to Melbourne on the [steamboat](/wiki/Steamboat) City of Melbourne, together with de Mestre, and two of de Mestre's other horses Exeter and Inheritor.[[33]](#cite_note-auto1-33) Before being winched aboard the steamboat for the trip to Melbourne, the horses had arrived in Sydney in September 1861.[[27]](#cite_note-masters-27)[[34]](#cite_note-Boat-34)

Archer travelled to Melbourne by steamboat again the following year (1862) to run in the second Melbourne Cup. This time he won 810 gold sovereigns (£810) cash and a gold watch before a crowd of 7,000, nearly twice the size of the previous years large crowd[[35]](#cite_note-weight-35) in a time of 3.47.00, taking to two the number of Melbourne Cup wins by this horse. Archer had already won the 1862 [AJC Queen Elizabeth Stakes](/wiki/AJC_Queen_Elizabeth_Stakes) in [Randwick](/wiki/Randwick_Racecourse), Sydney, and returned to win his second Melbourne Cup carrying 10 stone 2 pounds. He defeated a field of twenty starters by eight lengths, a record that has never been beaten, and that was not matched for over 100 years. Mormon again running second. Winning the Melbourne Cup twice was a feat not repeated until more than seventy years later when [Peter Pan](/wiki/Peter_Pan_III) won the race in 1932 and 1934, and winning the Melbourne Cup two years in a row was a feat not repeated until more than 30 years later when [Rain Lover](/wiki/Rain_Lover) won in 1968 and 1969.

Archer travelled to Melbourne by steamboat yet again the next year (1863). Despite his weight of 11 stone 4 pounds, Archer would have contested the third cup in 1863, but due to a Victorian public holiday trainer Etienne de Mestre's telegraphed acceptance form arrived late, and Archer was scratched on a technicality. In protest of this decision and in a show of solidarity, many of de Mestre's owners boycotted the third race and scratched their horses in sympathy.[[36]](#cite_note-36) As a result, the Melbourne Cup of that year ran with only 7 starters, the smallest number in the history of the Cup.

In 1865, Adam Lindsay Gordon wrote a verse in which the Melbourne Cup winner was called Tim Whiffler. Two years later in 1867 two horses with the name Tim Whiffler ran in the Melbourne Cup. (The year before in 1866 two horses with the same name, Falcon, also ran in the Melbourne Cup.) To distinguish between the two Tim Whifflers they were called "Sydney" Tim Whiffler and "Melbourne" Tim Whiffler. "Sydney" Tim Whiffler actually won the Cup.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-auto2-38) He was trained by Etienne de Mestre, and like Archer before him raced in de Mestre's name but was leased from the "Exeter Farm".[[30]](#cite_note-Hall-30)[[31]](#cite_note-book-31)

As early as 1865, Cup day was a half-holiday in Melbourne for public servants and bank officials. Various businesses also closed at lunchtime.

It took some years before the purpose of the declared holiday was acknowledged in the Victoria Government Gazette. The Gazette of 31 October 1873 announced that the following Thursday (Cup Day) be observed as a bank and civil (public) service holiday.[[33]](#cite_note-auto1-33)

The Melbourne Cup was first run on a Tuesday in 1875, the first Tuesday in that month.[[39]](#cite_note-facts-39)

On 7 November 1876, the three-year-old filly, [Briseis](/wiki/Briseis_(Australian_horse)), owned and trained by [James Wilson Snr.](/w/index.php?title=James_Wilson_Snr.&action=edit&redlink=1), won in a time of 3.36.25. Briseis then went on to create a record that is never likely to be equalled, winning the VRC Derby, the Melbourne Cup and the VRC Oaks in the space of six days. She was ridden in the Melbourne Cup by the tiny featherweight figure of jockey [Peter St Albans](/wiki/Peter_St_Albans). In 1876 at the recorded age thirteen (he was actually twelve, being 8 days short of his thirteenth birthday),[[40]](#cite_note-peter-40) Peter St Albans is also the youngest person ever to win a Melbourne Cup. Before 75,000 at [Flemington](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse) Briseis, with St Albans in the saddle, comfortably won by 1 length in the biggest field of all time. "At 4 o'clock the starter released the 33 runners and they swept down the long Flemington straight in a thundering rush. Briseis, ridden by what one writer termed a mere child, (in the Cup) captured a rare double, the Victoria Race Club Derby and the Melbourne Cup. Shouts and hurrahs were heard, hats were thrown in the air and one excited individual fell on his back in the attempt to do a somersault. The boy who rode the winner was carried around the pack and is the hero of the day," reported the "Australasian Sketcher" in 1876. Both Peter St Albans and Briseis have now become racing legends, and Briseis is regarded as one of the greatest mares foaled in Australia.

Briseis wasn't the only sensation surrounding the 1876 Melbourne Cup. Two months before the event, on Saturday 9 September, the City of Melbourne sailed for Melbourne from Sydney with a cargo including 13 racehorses, many of whom were considered serious contenders for the Melbourne Cup. The following day the ship ran into a savage storm and was hit by several rogue waves, with Nemesis (the winner of the 1876 [AJC Metropolitan Handicap](/wiki/AJC_Metropolitan_Handicap) in Randwick, Sydney and favourite for the Cup, owned by John Moffat) and Robin Hood (another favourite, owned by Etienne de Mestre) being among the 11 horses that were killed.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Betting on the big race was paralysed. To the dismay and anger of the public, bookmakers, showing no feeling, presented a purse (loaded with coins) to the captain as token of their appreciation for his part in saving them many thousands of pounds in bets already laid on the favourites who had perished. Perhaps they should have kept their money, however. The outsider Briseis comfortably won by 1 length in the biggest field of all time and in an extremely good time, so it is unlikely that the horses who perished could have beaten her.

1877 is also the year that the trainer Etienne de Mestre won his fourth Melbourne Cup with [Chester](/wiki/Chester_(horse)) owned by Hon. James White. In 1878, as in previous years, De Mestre fielded more than one horse. He entered the favourite Firebell (owned by W. S. Cox) who finished last, Chester (owned by Hon. James White) the previous year's winner who fell, and Calamia (owned by de Mestre) who, though less fancied, won easily[[31]](#cite_note-book-31) by two lengths. First prize was £1,790, the crowd was 80,000 and there were 30 starters. De Mestre's 1878 win with Calamia brought to 5 the number of Melbourne Cups he had won.[[35]](#cite_note-weight-35) This record was not to be matched for nearly 100 years when the trainer [Bart Cummings](/wiki/Bart_Cummings) won his fifth Melbourne Cup in 1975. Bart Cummings, regarded as the best Australian horse trainer of all time, went on to win 12 Melbourne Cups to 2008.

/wiki/File:MARTINI-HENRY_NZ.jpgMartini-Henry, the 1883 Melbourne Cup winner
In 1883, the hardy New Zealand bred, [Martini-Henry](/wiki/Martini-Henry_(horse)) won the VRC Derby, the Melbourne Cup and on the following Monday retained his undefeated record by winning Mares' Produce Stakes.[[42]](#cite_note-Cavanough-42)

/wiki/File:Pharlap1930melbournecup.jpg[Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap) winning the Melbourne Cup Race from Second Wind and Shadow King on 5 November 1930
[Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap), the most famous horse in the world of his day,[[43]](#cite_note-43) won the 1930 Melbourne Cup at 11/8 odds on, the shortest-priced favourite in the history of the race. He had to be hidden away at Geelong before the race after an attempt was made to shoot him and only emerged an hour before the race time of the Cup.[[44]](#cite_note-Flemington-44) Phar Lap also competed in 1929 and 1931, but came 3rd and 8th respectively, despite heavy favouritism in both years.

There are a few legends of the first [Aboriginal](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian) jockey to ride in a Melbourne Cup. It was believed to be [John Cutts](/wiki/John_Cutts_(jockey)) who won the first and second cups in 1861 and 1862 riding [archer](/wiki/Archer_(horse)). He was reputedly an Aboriginal stockman born in the area where Archer was trained but was actually John 'Cutts' Dillon, the son of a Sydney clerk, a jockey who rode for many trainers in his long career, and who was one of the best known, best-liked and most respected jockeys in New South Wales.[[45]](#cite_note-45) It is thought that [Peter St Albans](/wiki/Peter_St_Albans) was the first Aboriginal jockey to win the cup, on [Briseis](/wiki/Briseis_(Australian_horse)) in 1876. Because St Albans was not quite 13 years old, the jockey was too young to ride in the cup. Thus, to allow him to race Briseis in the Cup, it was argued his birthdate and parents were unknown, and from this, the legend of him being Aboriginal grew.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[40]](#cite_note-peter-40) Both these legends, however, can definitely be disproved, and history had to wait nearly another 100 years. The first jockey of [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian) heritage to ride a Melbourne Cup winner was [Frank Reys](/wiki/Frank_Reys) in 1973 on [Gala Supreme](/wiki/Gala_Supreme), who had a [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_people) father and a half-Aboriginal mother.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The race has undergone several alterations in recent years, the most visible being the entry of many foreign-trained horses. Most have failed to cope with the conditions; the three successful "foreign raids" include two by [Irish](/wiki/Ireland) trainer [Dermot K. Weld](/wiki/Dermot_K._Weld) successful in 1993 and 2002,[[48]](#cite_note-48) and one in 2006[[49]](#cite_note-2006_Melbourne_Cup_result-49) by [Katsumi Yoshida](/wiki/Katsumi_Yoshida) of [Japan](/wiki/Japan)'s renowned Yoshida racing and breeding family. The attraction for foreigners to compete was, primarily, the low-profile change to the new "quality handicap" weighting system. The 1910 Melbourne Cup was won by Comedy King, the first foreign bred horse to do so. Subsequent foreign-bred horses to win Cup were Backwood, 1924; [Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap), 1930; Wotan, 1936; [Beldale Ball](/wiki/Beldale_Ball), 1980; [At Talaq](/wiki/At_Talaq_(racehorse)), 1986; [Kingston Rule](/wiki/Kingston_Rule), 1990; [Vintage Crop](/wiki/Vintage_Crop), 1993; [Jeune](/wiki/Jeune), 1994; [Media Puzzle](/wiki/Media_Puzzle), 2002; [Makybe Diva](/wiki/Makybe_Diva), 2003, 2004, 2005; [Americain](/wiki/Americain_(racehorse)), 2010; and [Dunaden](/wiki/Dunaden), 2011.

The 1938 Melbourne Cup was won by [trainer](/wiki/Horse_trainer) [Mrs. Allan McDonald](/wiki/Hedwick_Wilhelmina_McDonald), who conditioned [Catalogue](/w/index.php?title=Catalogue_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1). Mrs McDonald was a successful trainer in New Zealand;[[50]](#cite_note-50) however, at the time, women were not allowed to compete as trainers in Australia, so her husband's name was officially recorded as the winning trainer.[[51]](#cite_note-Peake-51) The [2001 edition](/wiki/2001_Melbourne_Cup) was won by New Zealand mare [Ethereal](/wiki/Ethereal_(horse)),[[52]](#cite_note-52) trained by [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon), the first woman to formally train a Melbourne Cup winner. She also won the [Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup), a 2,400-metre race also held in Melbourne, and therefore has won the "Cups Double".

Maree Lyndon became the first female to ride in the Melbourne Cup, when she partnered Argonaut Style in 1987, in which she ran second-last place in the 21-horse field.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In 2004, [Makybe Diva](/wiki/Makybe_Diva)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) became the first [mare](/wiki/Mare_(horse)) to win two cups, and also the first horse to win with different trainers, after [David Hall](/wiki/David_Hall_(horse_trainer)) moved to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong) and transferred her to the [Lee Freedman](/wiki/Lee_Freedman) stables.

The [2005 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2005_Melbourne_Cup) was held before a crowd of 106,479. Makybe Diva made history by becoming the only horse to win the race three times. Trainer [Lee Freedman](/wiki/Lee_Freedman) said after the race, "Go and find the youngest child on the course because that's the only person here who will have a chance of seeing this happen again in their lifetime."

Due to the [2007 Australian equine influenza outbreak](/wiki/2007_Australian_equine_influenza_outbreak), believed to have been started by a horse brought into Australia from Japan, neither [Delta Blues](/wiki/Delta_Blues_(horse)) nor [Pop Rock](/wiki/Pop_Rock_(horse)) participated in the [2007 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2007_Melbourne_Cup). Both horses had been stabled in Japan. Corowa, NSW-trained "Leica Falcon" also was not permitted to race in Victoria despite Corowa being close to the Victorian border. Leica Falcon was ordained as the new staying star of Australian racing in 2005 when he ran fourth in both the Caulfield Cup and in Makybe Diva's famous third Melbourne Cup victory. But serious leg injuries saw the horse not race for another 20 months.[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Efficient](/wiki/Efficient_(horse)), the previous year's VRC Derby winner, won the race.

In 2013, [Damien Oliver](/wiki/Damien_Oliver) returned from an eight-month ban, after betting against his own mount at a previous race meet, to win his 3rd Melbourne cup.

/wiki/File:Flemington-race-course-180-degree.jpg140-degree panorama of the racecourse

In 2016, ABC Bullion, a [Pallion](/wiki/Pallion_Group) company
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="81db5ecee59ad295">>>"
}

Melbourne Cup/wiki/File:2013_Melbourne_Cup_(10705853945).jpgMelbourne Cup 2013

ClassGroup 1
Location[Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse)
[Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne), [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia)), Australia
Inaugurated1861; 165 years ago ([List of Melbourne Cup winners](/wiki/List_of_Melbourne_Cup_winners))
Race type[Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) – [Flat racing](/wiki/Flat_racing)
Sponsor[Lexus](/wiki/Lexus) (2025)
Race information
Distance3,200 m (1 mi 1,740 yd)
SurfaceTurf
TrackLeft-handed
QualificationThree-year-olds and up
WeightHandicap
Purse[A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)10 million (US$6.5 million) (2025)

The Melbourne Cup is an annual Group 1 [Thoroughbred horse race](/wiki/Thoroughbred_horse_racing) held in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne), [Australia](/wiki/Australia), at the [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse). It is a 3200-metre race for three-year-olds and older, conducted by the [Victoria Racing Club](/wiki/Victoria_Racing_Club) that forms part of the [Melbourne Spring Racing Carnival](/wiki/Melbourne_Spring_Racing_Carnival). It is the richest two-mile [handicap](/wiki/Handicap_(horse_racing)) in the world and one of the richest turf races. The event starts at 3:00 pm on the first Tuesday of November and is known locally as "the race that stops the nation".[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The Melbourne Cup has cemented itself as a part of [Melbourne](/wiki/Culture_of_Melbourne) and Australian culture, having been run every year since 1861. The day of the race has been a [public holiday](/wiki/Public_holidays_in_Australia) for much of Victoria since 1876.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was originally run over two mi (3.219 km) but was shortened to 3,200 m (1 mi 1,740 yd) in 1972 when [Australia adopted the metric system](/wiki/Metrication_in_Australia). This reduced the distance by 18.688 m (61 ft 33⁄4 in), and [Rain Lover](/wiki/Rain_Lover)'s 1968 race record of 3:19.1 was accordingly adjusted to 3:17.9. The present record holder is the 1990 winner [Kingston Rule](/wiki/Kingston_Rule) with a time of 3:16.3.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

## Qualifying and race conditions
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Melbourne_Cup&action=edit&section=1)]

/wiki/File:PETER_PAN_lll_VRC_MELBOURNE_CUP_1932_%26_1934.JPG[Peter Pan](/wiki/Peter_Pan_III), 1932 and 1934 winner
/wiki/File:Delta_1950_VRC_St_Leger_Flemington_Racecourse_Jockey_Neville_Sellwood_Trainer_Maurice_McCarten.jpg[Delta](/wiki/Delta_(horse)), 1951 winner
/wiki/File:Rising_Fast_(NZ).jpg [Rising Fast](/wiki/Rising_Fast), 1954 winner
/wiki/File:Russia_1946_VRC_Melbourne_Cup_Flemington_Racecourse_Jockey_Darby_Munro_Trainer_Ted_Hush.jpg[Russia](/wiki/Russia_(horse)), 1946 winner
/wiki/File:Dalray_1952_VRC_Melbourne_Cup_Jockey_Bill_Williamson_Trainer_Clarrie_McCarthy.jpg[Dalray](/wiki/Dalray), 1952 winner
The race is a quality handicap for horses three years old and older, run over a distance of 3200 metres, on the first Tuesday in November at Flemington Racecourse. The minimum handicap weight is 50kg. There is no maximum weight, but the top allocated weight must not be less than 57kg. The weight allocated to each horse is declared by the VRC Handicapper in early September.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

The Melbourne Cup race is a [handicap](/wiki/Handicapping) contest in which the weight of the [jockey](/wiki/Jockey) and riding gear is adjusted with ballast to a nominated figure. Older horses carry more weight than younger ones and weights are adjusted further according to the horse's previous results. The field is selected based on a range of factors, including each horse's age, weight, and previous racing performance.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Weights were theoretically calculated to give each horse an equal winning chance in the past, but in recent years the rules were adjusted to a "quality handicap" formula where superior horses are given less severe weight penalties than under pure handicap rules.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

After the declaration of weights for the Melbourne Cup, the winner of any handicap flat race of the advertised value of A$55,000 or over to the winner, or an internationally recognised Listed, Group, or Graded handicap flat race, shall carry such additional weight (if any), for each win, as the VRC Handicapper shall determine.

Entries for the Melbourne Cup usually close during the first week of August. The initial entry fee is $600 per horse. Around 300 to 400 horses are nominated each year, but the final field is limited to 24 starters. Following the allocation of weights, the owner of each horse must on the four occasions before the race in November declare the horse as an acceptor and pay a fee. First acceptance is $960, second acceptance is $1,450 and third acceptance is $2,420. The final acceptance fee, on the Saturday prior to the race, is $45,375. Should a horse be balloted out of the final field, the final declaration fee is refunded.

### Balloting conditions
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Melbourne_Cup&action=edit&section=4)]

The race directors may exclude any horse from the race or exempt any horse from the ballot on the race, but in order to reduce the field to the safety limit of 24, horses are balloted out based on a number of factors which include prize money earned in the previous two years, wins or placings in certain lead-up races and allocated handicap weight.

The winners of the following races are exempt from any ballot:[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)

- [Lexus Stakes](/wiki/Lexus_Stakes) (formerly Saab Quality and registered as The Hotham Handicap)

- [Cox Plate](/wiki/Cox_Plate)

- [Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup)

- [The Bart Cummings](/wiki/The_Bart_Cummings) (from 2015)

- Andrew Ramsden Stakes (from 2019)

- [Ebor Handicap](/wiki/Ebor_Handicap) (from 2019)

- The Roy Higgins Quality (from 2022)

- [Geelong Cup](/wiki/Geelong_Cup)

- [Moonee Valley Gold Cup](/wiki/Moonee_Valley_Gold_Cup)

- [Belmont Gold Cup Stakes](/wiki/Belmont_Gold_Cup_Stakes)

The limitation of 24 starters is stated explicitly to be for safety reasons. However, in the past far larger numbers were allowed – the largest field ever raced was 39 runners in 1890.

International horses (except from New Zealand) entering Australia must undergo quarantine in an approved premises in their own country for a minimum period of 14 days before travelling to Australia. Premises must meet the Australian Government Standards.[[9]](#cite_note-9) The Werribee International Horse Centre at Werribee Racecourse is the Victorian quarantine station for international horses competing in the [Melbourne Spring Racing Carnival](/wiki/Melbourne_Spring_Racing_Carnival). The facility has stabling for up to 24 horses in five separate stable complexes and is located 32 km from the Melbourne [CBD](/wiki/Central_business_district).[[10]](#cite_note-10)

## Prize money and trophies
[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Melbourne_Cup&action=edit&section=6)]

The total prize money for the 2023 race was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)8,410,000,[[11]](#cite_note-www.races.com.au-2023-11)[[12]](#cite_note-12) plus an 18-carat solid gold trophy weighing 1.69kg and valued at $600,000 to the winner.[[13]](#cite_note-13) The first 12 past the post receive prize money, with the winner being paid $4.4 million, second $1.1 million, third $550,000, fourth $350,000, fifth $230,000, with sixth through to twelfth place earning $160,000.[[14]](#cite_note-14) Prize money is distributed to the connections of each horse in the ratio of 85 percent to the owner, 10 percent to the trainer and 5 percent to the jockey.

The 1985 Melbourne Cup, won by What a Nuisance, was the first race run in Australia with prize money of $1 million.

Year

/wiki/File:76MelbCup.jpgThe 1976 cup won by [Van der Hum](/wiki/Van_der_Hum)
The winner of the first Melbourne Cup in 1861 received a gold watch. The first Melbourne Cup trophy was awarded in 1865 and was an elaborate silver bowl on a stand that had been manufactured in England.[[17]](#cite_note-nma-17) This is currently owned by Australian wine personality [Wolf Blass](/wiki/Wolf_Blass) and is on display at the Wolf Blass Gallery & Museum/Adelaide Hills Wine Bar in Hahndorf, South Australia. The first existing and un-altered Melbourne Cup is from 1866, presented to the owners of [The Barb](/wiki/The_Barb); as of 2013, it is in the [National Museum of Australia](/wiki/National_Museum_of_Australia).[[18]](#cite_note-nma1866-18) The silver trophy presented in 1867, now also in the National Museum of Australia,[[18]](#cite_note-nma1866-18) was also made in England, but jewellers in [Victoria](/wiki/Victoria_(Australia)) complained to the Victorian Racing Club that the trophy should have been made locally. They believed the work of a Melburnian, William Edwards, to be superior in both design and workmanship to the English-made trophy. No trophy was awarded to the Melbourne Cup winner for the next eight years.[[17]](#cite_note-nma-17)

In 1876, [Edward Fischer](/wiki/Edward_Fischer), an immigrant from Austria, produced the first Australian-made trophy. It was an Etruscan shape with two handles. One side depicted a horse race with the grandstand and hill of Flemington in the background. The opposite side had the words "Melbourne Cup, 1876" and the name of the winning horse.[[19]](#cite_note-loving_cup-19) A silver-plated base sporting three silver horses was added in 1888, but in 1891 the prize changed to being a 15 in-high (380 mm), 24 in-long (610 mm) trophy showing a Victory figure offering an olive wreath to a jockey.[[19]](#cite_note-loving_cup-19) From 1899, the trophy was in the form of silver galloping horse embossed on a 3 ft-long (0.9 m) plaque, although it was said to look like a [greyhound](/wiki/Greyhound) by some people.

The last Melbourne Cup trophy manufactured in England was made for the 1914 event. It was a chalice centred on a long base which had a horse at each end. The trophy awarded in 1916,[[20]](#cite_note-20) the first gold trophy, was a three-legged, three-armed rose bowl. The three-handled [loving cup](/wiki/Loving_cup) design was first awarded in 1919. In that year, the Victorian Racing Club had commissioned James Steeth to design a trophy that would be in keeping with the prestige of the race, little realising that it would become the iconic Melbourne Cup still presented today.[[21]](#cite_note-21) In the Second World War years (1942, 1943 and 1944), the winning owner received [war bonds](/wiki/War_bond) valued at 200 pounds.

A new trophy is struck each year and becomes the property of the winning owner. In the event of a dead heat, a second cup is on hand. The present trophy is hand spun from 1.65 kg of 18-carat gold.[[22]](#cite_note-22) The winning trainer and jockey also receive a miniature replica of the cup (since 1973) and the [strapper](/wiki/Strapper) is awarded the [Tommy Woodcock](/wiki/Tommy_Woodcock) Trophy, named after the strapper of [Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap).

In 2003, an annual tour of the Melbourne Cup trophy was initiated to provide communities across Australia and New Zealand with an opportunity to view the Cup trophy and highlight the contribution the Melbourne Cup has made to Australia's social, sporting and racing culture. Each year, communities in Australia and New Zealand apply for the cup to tour their community and the tour also takes in cities around the world as part of the Victoria Racing Club's strategy to promote the Melbourne Cup and the Melbourne Cup Carnival internationally.[[23]](#cite_note-23)

The Tour has visited schools and aged-care and hospital facilities, and participated in community events and celebrations including race days across Australia and New Zealand.[[24]](#cite_note-24)

/wiki/File:Melbourne_cup_1881.jpgMelbourne Cup in 1881, with engraving on the finish line

[Frederick Standish](/wiki/Frederick_Standish), member of the Victorian Turf Club and steward on the day of the first Cup,[[25]](#cite_note-essay-25) was credited with forming the idea to hold a horse race and calling it the "Melbourne Cup".[[25]](#cite_note-essay-25)[[26]](#cite_note-26)

Seventeen horses contested the first Melbourne Cup on Thursday 7 November 1861, racing for the modest prize of 710 [gold sovereigns](/wiki/Coins_of_Australia) ([£](/wiki/Pound_(currency))710) cash and a hand-beaten gold [watch](/wiki/Watch), winner takes all.[[27]](#cite_note-masters-27)[[28]](#cite_note-28) The prize was not, as some have suggested, the largest purse up to that time. A large crowd of 4,000 men and women watched the race, although it has been suggested this was less than expected because of news reaching Melbourne of the death of explorers [Burke and Wills](/wiki/Burke_and_Wills)[[25]](#cite_note-essay-25) five days earlier on 2 November. Nevertheless, the attendance was the largest at Flemington on any day for the past two years, with the exception of the recently run Two Thousand Guinea Stakes.[[27]](#cite_note-masters-27)

The winner of this first Melbourne Cup race was a 16.3 [hand](/wiki/Hand_(length)) [bay](/wiki/Bay_(horse)) [stallion](/wiki/Stallion) by the name of [Archer](/wiki/Archer_(horse)) in a time of 3.52.00, ridden by [John Cutts](/wiki/John_Cutts_(jockey)), trained by [Etienne de Mestre](/wiki/Etienne_L._de_Mestre), and leased (and consequently raced in his own name) by de Mestre. As a lessee de Mestre owned and was fully responsible for Archer during the lease. Archer was leased from the "Exeter Farm" of [Jembaicumbene](/wiki/Jembaicumbene) near [Braidwood, New South Wales](/wiki/Braidwood,_New_South_Wales). His owners were Thomas John "Tom" Roberts (a good school-friend of de Mestre's), Rowland H. Hassall (Roberts' brother-in-law), and Edmund Molyneux Royds and William Edward Royds (Roberts' nephews).[[29]](#cite_note-heritage-29)[[30]](#cite_note-Hall-30)[[31]](#cite_note-book-31)

The inaugural Melbourne Cup of 1861 was an eventful affair when one horse bolted before the start, and three of the seventeen starters fell during the race, two of which died. Archer, a Sydney outsider who drew scant favour in the betting, spread-eagled the field and defeated the favourite, and Victorian champion, Mormon by six lengths. Dismissed by the bookies, Archer took a lot of money away from Melbourne, 'refuelling interstate rivalry' and adding to the excitement of the Cup.[[32]](#cite_note-32) The next day, Archer was raced in and won another 2-mile long-distance race, the Melbourne Town Plate.

It has become legend that Archer walked over 800 km (over 500 miles) to Flemington from de Mestre's stable at "Terara" near [Nowra, New South Wales](/wiki/Nowra,_New_South_Wales). However, newspaper archives of the day reveal that he had travelled south from Sydney to Melbourne on the [steamboat](/wiki/Steamboat) City of Melbourne, together with de Mestre, and two of de Mestre's other horses Exeter and Inheritor.[[33]](#cite_note-auto1-33) Before being winched aboard the steamboat for the trip to Melbourne, the horses had arrived in Sydney in September 1861.[[27]](#cite_note-masters-27)[[34]](#cite_note-Boat-34)

Archer travelled to Melbourne by steamboat again the following year (1862) to run in the second Melbourne Cup. This time he won 810 gold sovereigns (£810) cash and a gold watch before a crowd of 7,000, nearly twice the size of the previous years large crowd[[35]](#cite_note-weight-35) in a time of 3.47.00, taking to two the number of Melbourne Cup wins by this horse. Archer had already won the 1862 [AJC Queen Elizabeth Stakes](/wiki/AJC_Queen_Elizabeth_Stakes) in [Randwick](/wiki/Randwick_Racecourse), Sydney, and returned to win his second Melbourne Cup carrying 10 stone 2 pounds. He defeated a field of twenty starters by eight lengths, a record that has never been beaten, and that was not matched for over 100 years. Mormon again running second. Winning the Melbourne Cup twice was a feat not repeated until more than seventy years later when [Peter Pan](/wiki/Peter_Pan_III) won the race in 1932 and 1934, and winning the Melbourne Cup two years in a row was a feat not repeated until more than 30 years later when [Rain Lover](/wiki/Rain_Lover) won in 1968 and 1969.

Archer travelled to Melbourne by steamboat yet again the next year (1863). Despite his weight of 11 stone 4 pounds, Archer would have contested the third cup in 1863, but due to a Victorian public holiday trainer Etienne de Mestre's telegraphed acceptance form arrived late, and Archer was scratched on a technicality. In protest of this decision and in a show of solidarity, many of de Mestre's owners boycotted the third race and scratched their horses in sympathy.[[36]](#cite_note-36) As a result, the Melbourne Cup of that year ran with only 7 starters, the smallest number in the history of the Cup.

In 1865, Adam Lindsay Gordon wrote a verse in which the Melbourne Cup winner was called Tim Whiffler. Two years later in 1867 two horses with the name Tim Whiffler ran in the Melbourne Cup. (The year before in 1866 two horses with the same name, Falcon, also ran in the Melbourne Cup.) To distinguish between the two Tim Whifflers they were called "Sydney" Tim Whiffler and "Melbourne" Tim Whiffler. "Sydney" Tim Whiffler actually won the Cup.[[37]](#cite_note-37)[[38]](#cite_note-auto2-38) He was trained by Etienne de Mestre, and like Archer before him raced in de Mestre's name but was leased from the "Exeter Farm".[[30]](#cite_note-Hall-30)[[31]](#cite_note-book-31)

As early as 1865, Cup day was a half-holiday in Melbourne for public servants and bank officials. Various businesses also closed at lunchtime.

It took some years before the purpose of the declared holiday was acknowledged in the Victoria Government Gazette. The Gazette of 31 October 1873 announced that the following Thursday (Cup Day) be observed as a bank and civil (public) service holiday.[[33]](#cite_note-auto1-33)

The Melbourne Cup was first run on a Tuesday in 1875, the first Tuesday in that month.[[39]](#cite_note-facts-39)

On 7 November 1876, the three-year-old filly, [Briseis](/wiki/Briseis_(Australian_horse)), owned and trained by [James Wilson Snr.](/w/index.php?title=James_Wilson_Snr.&action=edit&redlink=1), won in a time of 3.36.25. Briseis then went on to create a record that is never likely to be equalled, winning the VRC Derby, the Melbourne Cup and the VRC Oaks in the space of six days. She was ridden in the Melbourne Cup by the tiny featherweight figure of jockey [Peter St Albans](/wiki/Peter_St_Albans). In 1876 at the recorded age thirteen (he was actually twelve, being 8 days short of his thirteenth birthday),[[40]](#cite_note-peter-40) Peter St Albans is also the youngest person ever to win a Melbourne Cup. Before 75,000 at [Flemington](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse) Briseis, with St Albans in the saddle, comfortably won by 1 length in the biggest field of all time. "At 4 o'clock the starter released the 33 runners and they swept down the long Flemington straight in a thundering rush. Briseis, ridden by what one writer termed a mere child, (in the Cup) captured a rare double, the Victoria Race Club Derby and the Melbourne Cup. Shouts and hurrahs were heard, hats were thrown in the air and one excited individual fell on his back in the attempt to do a somersault. The boy who rode the winner was carried around the pack and is the hero of the day," reported the "Australasian Sketcher" in 1876. Both Peter St Albans and Briseis have now become racing legends, and Briseis is regarded as one of the greatest mares foaled in Australia.

Briseis wasn't the only sensation surrounding the 1876 Melbourne Cup. Two months before the event, on Saturday 9 September, the City of Melbourne sailed for Melbourne from Sydney with a cargo including 13 racehorses, many of whom were considered serious contenders for the Melbourne Cup. The following day the ship ran into a savage storm and was hit by several rogue waves, with Nemesis (the winner of the 1876 [AJC Metropolitan Handicap](/wiki/AJC_Metropolitan_Handicap) in Randwick, Sydney and favourite for the Cup, owned by John Moffat) and Robin Hood (another favourite, owned by Etienne de Mestre) being among the 11 horses that were killed.[[41]](#cite_note-41) Betting on the big race was paralysed. To the dismay and anger of the public, bookmakers, showing no feeling, presented a purse (loaded with coins) to the captain as token of their appreciation for his part in saving them many thousands of pounds in bets already laid on the favourites who had perished. Perhaps they should have kept their money, however. The outsider Briseis comfortably won by 1 length in the biggest field of all time and in an extremely good time, so it is unlikely that the horses who perished could have beaten her.

1877 is also the year that the trainer Etienne de Mestre won his fourth Melbourne Cup with [Chester](/wiki/Chester_(horse)) owned by Hon. James White. In 1878, as in previous years, De Mestre fielded more than one horse. He entered the favourite Firebell (owned by W. S. Cox) who finished last, Chester (owned by Hon. James White) the previous year's winner who fell, and Calamia (owned by de Mestre) who, though less fancied, won easily[[31]](#cite_note-book-31) by two lengths. First prize was £1,790, the crowd was 80,000 and there were 30 starters. De Mestre's 1878 win with Calamia brought to 5 the number of Melbourne Cups he had won.[[35]](#cite_note-weight-35) This record was not to be matched for nearly 100 years when the trainer [Bart Cummings](/wiki/Bart_Cummings) won his fifth Melbourne Cup in 1975. Bart Cummings, regarded as the best Australian horse trainer of all time, went on to win 12 Melbourne Cups to 2008.

/wiki/File:MARTINI-HENRY_NZ.jpgMartini-Henry, the 1883 Melbourne Cup winner
In 1883, the hardy New Zealand bred, [Martini-Henry](/wiki/Martini-Henry_(horse)) won the VRC Derby, the Melbourne Cup and on the following Monday retained his undefeated record by winning Mares' Produce Stakes.[[42]](#cite_note-Cavanough-42)

/wiki/File:Pharlap1930melbournecup.jpg[Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap) winning the Melbourne Cup Race from Second Wind and Shadow King on 5 November 1930
[Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap), the most famous horse in the world of his day,[[43]](#cite_note-43) won the 1930 Melbourne Cup at 11/8 odds on, the shortest-priced favourite in the history of the race. He had to be hidden away at Geelong before the race after an attempt was made to shoot him and only emerged an hour before the race time of the Cup.[[44]](#cite_note-Flemington-44) Phar Lap also competed in 1929 and 1931, but came 3rd and 8th respectively, despite heavy favouritism in both years.

There are a few legends of the first [Aboriginal](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian) jockey to ride in a Melbourne Cup. It was believed to be [John Cutts](/wiki/John_Cutts_(jockey)) who won the first and second cups in 1861 and 1862 riding [archer](/wiki/Archer_(horse)). He was reputedly an Aboriginal stockman born in the area where Archer was trained but was actually John 'Cutts' Dillon, the son of a Sydney clerk, a jockey who rode for many trainers in his long career, and who was one of the best known, best-liked and most respected jockeys in New South Wales.[[45]](#cite_note-45) It is thought that [Peter St Albans](/wiki/Peter_St_Albans) was the first Aboriginal jockey to win the cup, on [Briseis](/wiki/Briseis_(Australian_horse)) in 1876. Because St Albans was not quite 13 years old, the jockey was too young to ride in the cup. Thus, to allow him to race Briseis in the Cup, it was argued his birthdate and parents were unknown, and from this, the legend of him being Aboriginal grew.[[46]](#cite_note-46)[[40]](#cite_note-peter-40) Both these legends, however, can definitely be disproved, and history had to wait nearly another 100 years. The first jockey of [Indigenous](/wiki/Indigenous_Australian) heritage to ride a Melbourne Cup winner was [Frank Reys](/wiki/Frank_Reys) in 1973 on [Gala Supreme](/wiki/Gala_Supreme), who had a [Filipino](/wiki/Filipino_people) father and a half-Aboriginal mother.[[47]](#cite_note-47)

The race has undergone several alterations in recent years, the most visible being the entry of many foreign-trained horses. Most have failed to cope with the conditions; the three successful "foreign raids" include two by [Irish](/wiki/Ireland) trainer [Dermot K. Weld](/wiki/Dermot_K._Weld) successful in 1993 and 2002,[[48]](#cite_note-48) and one in 2006[[49]](#cite_note-2006_Melbourne_Cup_result-49) by [Katsumi Yoshida](/wiki/Katsumi_Yoshida) of [Japan](/wiki/Japan)'s renowned Yoshida racing and breeding family. The attraction for foreigners to compete was, primarily, the low-profile change to the new "quality handicap" weighting system. The 1910 Melbourne Cup was won by Comedy King, the first foreign bred horse to do so. Subsequent foreign-bred horses to win Cup were Backwood, 1924; [Phar Lap](/wiki/Phar_Lap), 1930; Wotan, 1936; [Beldale Ball](/wiki/Beldale_Ball), 1980; [At Talaq](/wiki/At_Talaq_(racehorse)), 1986; [Kingston Rule](/wiki/Kingston_Rule), 1990; [Vintage Crop](/wiki/Vintage_Crop), 1993; [Jeune](/wiki/Jeune), 1994; [Media Puzzle](/wiki/Media_Puzzle), 2002; [Makybe Diva](/wiki/Makybe_Diva), 2003, 2004, 2005; [Americain](/wiki/Americain_(racehorse)), 2010; and [Dunaden](/wiki/Dunaden), 2011.

The 1938 Melbourne Cup was won by [trainer](/wiki/Horse_trainer) [Mrs. Allan McDonald](/wiki/Hedwick_Wilhelmina_McDonald), who conditioned [Catalogue](/w/index.php?title=Catalogue_(horse)&action=edit&redlink=1). Mrs McDonald was a successful trainer in New Zealand;[[50]](#cite_note-50) however, at the time, women were not allowed to compete as trainers in Australia, so her husband's name was officially recorded as the winning trainer.[[51]](#cite_note-Peake-51) The [2001 edition](/wiki/2001_Melbourne_Cup) was won by New Zealand mare [Ethereal](/wiki/Ethereal_(horse)),[[52]](#cite_note-52) trained by [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon), the first woman to formally train a Melbourne Cup winner. She also won the [Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup), a 2,400-metre race also held in Melbourne, and therefore has won the "Cups Double".

Maree Lyndon became the first female to ride in the Melbourne Cup, when she partnered Argonaut Style in 1987, in which she ran second-last place in the 21-horse field.[[53]](#cite_note-53)

In 2004, [Makybe Diva](/wiki/Makybe_Diva)[[54]](#cite_note-54)[[55]](#cite_note-55) became the first [mare](/wiki/Mare_(horse)) to win two cups, and also the first horse to win with different trainers, after [David Hall](/wiki/David_Hall_(horse_trainer)) moved to [Hong Kong](/wiki/Hong_Kong) and transferred her to the [Lee Freedman](/wiki/Lee_Freedman) stables.

The [2005 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2005_Melbourne_Cup) was held before a crowd of 106,479. Makybe Diva made history by becoming the only horse to win the race three times. Trainer [Lee Freedman](/wiki/Lee_Freedman) said after the race, "Go and find the youngest child on the course because that's the only person here who will have a chance of seeing this happen again in their lifetime."

Due to the [2007 Australian equine influenza outbreak](/wiki/2007_Australian_equine_influenza_outbreak), believed to have been started by a horse brought into Australia from Japan, neither [Delta Blues](/wiki/Delta_Blues_(horse)) nor [Pop Rock](/wiki/Pop_Rock_(horse)) participated in the [2007 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2007_Melbourne_Cup). Both horses had been stabled in Japan. Corowa, NSW-trained "Leica Falcon" also was not permitted to race in Victoria despite Corowa being close to the Victorian border. Leica Falcon was ordained as the new staying star of Australian racing in 2005 when he ran fourth in both the Caulfield Cup and in Makybe Diva's famous third Melbourne Cup victory. But serious leg injuries saw the horse not race for another 20 months.[[56]](#cite_note-56) [Efficient](/wiki/Efficient_(horse)), the previous year's VRC Derby winner, won the race.

In 2013, [Damien Oliver](/wiki/Damien_Oliver) returned from an eight-month ban, after betting against his own mount at a previous race meet, to win his 3rd Melbourne cup.

/wiki/File:Flemington-race-course-180-degree.jpg140-degree panorama of the racecourse

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This is a list of the winners of the Melbourne Cup. The [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup) is Australia's major [thoroughbred horse race](/wiki/Thoroughbred_racing), which is run at [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse)[[1]](#cite_note-1) in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne). Each year, internationally bred or owned horses compete in the race. Since 1882, New Zealand–bred horses have won 44 Melbourne Cups, British-bred horses have won seven, Irish-bred horses have won six, American-bred horses have won five, German-bred and French-bred horses have won two each, and Japanese-bred horses have won one.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Note: Horses and jockeys are Australian unless mentioned

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

This is a list of the winners of the Melbourne Cup. The [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup) is Australia's major [thoroughbred horse race](/wiki/Thoroughbred_racing), which is run at [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse)[[1]](#cite_note-1) in [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne). Each year, internationally bred or owned horses compete in the race. Since 1882, New Zealand–bred horses have won 44 Melbourne Cups, British-bred horses have won seven, Irish-bred horses have won six, American-bred horses have won five, German-bred and French-bred horses have won two each, and Japanese-bred horses have won one.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

Note: Horses and jockeys are Australian unless mentioned

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

2024 Melbourne Cup[Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup)
/wiki/File:Flemington-race-course-180-degree.jpg[Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse), location of the race

Location[Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse)
[Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne), Australia
Date5 November 2024
Distance3,200 metres
Winning horse[Knight's Choice](/wiki/Knight%27s_Choice)
[Starting price](/wiki/Starting_price)$81
[Jockey](/wiki/Jockey)[Robbie Dolan](/wiki/Robbie_Dolan)
[Trainer](/wiki/Horse_trainer)John Symons & [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon)
SurfaceGrass
Attendance91,168
← [2023](/wiki/2023_Melbourne_Cup)

[2025](/wiki/2025_Melbourne_Cup) →

Land Legend goes to the front with Okita Sushi on the outside, then Zardozi, back behind those Circle of Fire, Fancy Man and from a long way back Buckaroo. It's Okita Sushi, Zardozi a length away on the inside wearing it down. Okita Sushi grabbed by Zardozi, Knight's Choice is coming through at any old price and Warp Speed as well. Zardozi grabbed by Knight's Choice and Warp Speed coming at it. Knight's Choice, Warp Speed they hit the line, it's a photo finish! Knight's Choice I reckon's won from Warp Speed in a photo, what a magnificent finish to the Cup!

Commentator [Matt Hill](/wiki/Matt_Hill_(race_caller)) describes the climax of the race[[1]](#cite_note-1)

The 2024 Melbourne Cup, known commercially as the 2024 Lexus Melbourne Cup, was the 164th running of the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup), an Australian [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) horse race. The race, run over 3,200 metres (1.988 mi), was held on 5 November 2024 at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne)'s [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The final field for the race was declared on 2 November 2024.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The total prize money for the race was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)8.56 million, a $150,000 increase from 2023.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It was the first Melbourne Cup to be broadcast by [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network) after [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10), who had broadcast the race since [2019](/wiki/2019_Melbourne_Cup), dropped out of the bidding for the rights to the race in June 2023. Nine Network will have broadcast rights from 2024 until 2029.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The Italian jockey [Frankie Dettori](/wiki/Frankie_Dettori) decided to not retire from horse racing in order to compete in the 2024 Cup. He was ruled out of the 2023 Cup after being suspended for a whip breach.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The race was won by [Knight's Choice](/wiki/Knight%27s_Choice), ridden by Robbie Dolan. This was his first time racing in the Cup.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Knight’s Choice was co-trained by [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon) who trained the [2001 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2001_Melbourne_Cup) winner [Ethereal](/wiki/Ethereal_(horse)). She was also involved with [1988](/wiki/1988_Melbourne_Cup) winner [Empire Rose](/wiki/Empire_Rose), who was trained by Laurie Laxon.

Land Legend goes to the front with Okita Sushi on the outside, then Zardozi, back behind those Circle of Fire, Fancy Man and from a long way back Buckaroo. It's Okita Sushi, Zardozi a length away on the inside wearing it down. Okita Sushi grabbed by Zardozi, Knight's Choice is coming through at any old price and Warp Speed as well. Zardozi grabbed by Knight's Choice and Warp Speed coming at it. Knight's Choice, Warp Speed they hit the line, it's a photo finish! The 2024 Melbourne Cup, known commercially as the 2024 Lexus Melbourne Cup, was the 164th running of the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup), an Australian [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) horse race. The race, run over 3,200 metres (1.988 mi), was held on 5 November 2024 at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne)'s [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse).[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The final field for the race was declared on 2 November 2024.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The total prize money for the race was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)8.56 million, a $150,000 increase from 2023.[[4]](#cite_note-4) It was the first Melbourne Cup to be broadcast by [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network) after [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10), who had broadcast the race since [2019](/wiki/2019_Melbourne_Cup), dropped out of the bidding for the rights to the race in June 2023. Nine Network will have broadcast rights from 2024 until 2029.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)[[8]](#cite_note-8)[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The Italian jockey [Frankie Dettori](/wiki/Frankie_Dettori) decided to not retire from horse racing in order to compete in the 2024 Cup. He was ruled out of the 2023 Cup after being suspended for a whip breach.[[10]](#cite_note-10)

The race was won by [Knight's Choice](/wiki/Knight%27s_Choice), ridden by Robbie Dolan. This was his first time racing in the Cup.[[11]](#cite_note-11) Knight’s Choice was co-trained by [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon) who trained the [2001 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2001_Melbourne_Cup) winner [Ethereal](/wiki/Ethereal_(horse)). She was also involved with [1988](/wiki/1988_Melbourne_Cup) winner [Empire Rose](/wiki/Empire_Rose), who was trained by Laurie Laxon.

400 meters to go, it's Absurde moving up on the outside for Zac Purton a length in front but Without A Fight is running on right down the centre of the track. Without A Fight up to Absurde, Vow And Declare and Sheraz the bolter. It's Without A Fight, a hundred meters to go, 2 or 3 lengths in front, coming away from Sheraz and also Soulcome. The 2023 Melbourne Cup, known commercially as the 2023 Lexus Melbourne Cup, was the 163rd running of the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup), an Australian [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) horse race. The race, run over 3,200 metres (1.988 mi), was held on 7 November 2023 at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne)'s [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse).

The final field for the race was declared on 4 November 2023.[[1]](#cite_note-1) The total prize money for the race was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)8.41 million, a $410,000 increase from 2022.[[2]](#cite_note-2) It was the last Melbourne Cup broadcast by [Network 10](/wiki/Network_10), who have broadcast the race since [2019](/wiki/2019_Melbourne_Cup) and dropped out of the bidding for the broadcast rights in June 2023.[[3]](#cite_note-3)[[4]](#cite_note-4) In February 2024, [Nine Entertainment](/wiki/Nine_Entertainment), owner of [Nine Network](/wiki/Nine_Network), announced they will broadcast the race from 2024 until 2029.[[5]](#cite_note-5)[[6]](#cite_note-6)[[7]](#cite_note-7)

The race was won by [Without A Fight](/wiki/Without_A_Fight_(horse)), ridden by [Mark Zahra](/wiki/Mark_Zahra).[[8]](#cite_note-8) It was Zahra's second Melbourne Cup win, having ridden [Gold Trip](/wiki/Gold_Trip) to victory in the [2022 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2022_Melbourne_Cup).[[9]](#cite_note-9)

The top-weighted horse was the 17th placed [Gold Trip](/wiki/Gold_Trip) carrying 58kg, then Alenquer (21st), and the winner [Without A Fight](/wiki/Without_A_Fight_(horse)) on 56.5kg.

[2023](/wiki/2023_Melbourne_Cup) →

Gold Trip moves up takes the lead from Deauville Legend, 250 metres to go, Emissary running on. Gold Trip a hundred and fifty metres to go wandering about getting tired Emissary is trying very very hard. But Gold Trip is brave, a hundred to go, a length and a half Emissary. Gold Trip is going to win the Lexus Melbourne Cup! Gold Trip wins it in a real staying contest for the ages, two lengths Emissary, late third High Emocean.

Commentator Matt Hill describes the climax of the race

The 2022 Melbourne Cup (known commercially as the 2022 Lexus Melbourne Cup) was the 162nd running of the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup), a prestigious Australian [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) horse race. The race, run over 3,200 metres (1.988 mi), was held on 1 November 2022 at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne)'s [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse).

The final field for the race was declared on 29 October 2022.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The total prize money for the race was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)8 million, the same as the previous year.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The race was won by [Gold Trip](/wiki/Gold_Trip), ridden by [Mark Zahra](/wiki/Mark_Zahra) and trained by [Ciaron Maher](/wiki/Ciaron_Maher) & David Eustace.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

At least three Melbourne Cup runners were ruled out of the races during failed CT scan, including [Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup) winner Durston.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

2022 Melbourne Cup[Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup)
/wiki/File:Flemington-race-course-180-degree.jpg[Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse), location of the race.

Location[Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse)
[Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne), Australia
Date1 November 2022

[Starting price](/wiki/Starting_price)$21
[Jockey](/wiki/Jockey)[Mark Zahra](/wiki/Mark_Zahra)
[Trainer](/wiki/Horse_trainer)[Ciaron Maher](/wiki/Ciaron_Maher) & David Eustace
SurfaceGrass
Attendance73,816[[1]](#cite_note-1)
← [2021](/wiki/2021_Melbourne_Cup)

[2023](/wiki/2023_Melbourne_Cup) →

Gold Trip moves up takes the lead from Deauville Legend, 250 metres to go, Emissary running on. Gold Trip a hundred and fifty metres to go wandering about getting tired Emissary is trying very very hard. But Gold Trip is brave, a hundred to go, a length and a half Emissary. Gold Trip is going to win the Lexus Melbourne Cup! Gold Trip wins it in a real staying contest for the ages, two lengths Emissary, late third High Emocean.

Commentator Matt Hill describes the climax of the race

The 2022 Melbourne Cup (known commercially as the 2022 Lexus Melbourne Cup) was the 162nd running of the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup), a prestigious Australian [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) horse race. The race, run over 3,200 metres (1.988 mi), was held on 1 November 2022 at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne)'s [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse).

The final field for the race was declared on 29 October 2022.[[2]](#cite_note-2) The total prize money for the race was [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)8 million, the same as the previous year.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

The race was won by [Gold Trip](/wiki/Gold_Trip), ridden by [Mark Zahra](/wiki/Mark_Zahra) and trained by [Ciaron Maher](/wiki/Ciaron_Maher) & David Eustace.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

At least three Melbourne Cup runners were ruled out of the races during failed CT scan, including [Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup) winner Durston.[[5]](#cite_note-5)

300 meters to go, Goodie Two Shoes for Ireland led by a length and a quarter, Middle Earth and Half Yours is getting through. Goodie Two Shoes with Half Yours challenging at the clock tower. Half Yours takes the lead from Goodie Two Shoes, Middle Earth, River of Stars. Half Yours is coming clear, for Jamie Kah for the Cups Double. Half Yours the history-maker, won the Lexus Melbourne Cup by three lengths!

Commentator [Matt Hill](/wiki/Matt_Hill_(race_caller)) describes the climax of the race

The 2025 Melbourne Cup, known commercially as the 2025 Lexus Melbourne Cup, was the 165th running of the [Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup), an Australian [thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) horse race. The race, run over 3,200 metres (1.988 mi), was held on 4 November 2025 at [Melbourne](/wiki/Melbourne)'s [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse).[[1]](#cite_note-1)[[2]](#cite_note-2)

The final field for the race was declared on 1 November 2025.[[3]](#cite_note-3) The total prize money for the race is [A$](/wiki/Australian_dollar)10 million, a $144,000 increase from 2024.[[4]](#cite_note-4) The race was won by Half Yours, ridden by [Jamie Melham](/wiki/Jamie_Melham), the first woman to win since [Michelle Payne](/wiki/Michelle_Payne) in [2015](/wiki/2015_Melbourne_Cup).[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Half Yours (foaled 10 October 2020) is a [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse).[[1]](#cite_note-breednet.com.au-1) Based in [Ballarat](/wiki/Ballarat), Victoria,[[2]](#cite_note-racenet.com.au-2) he won the [2025 Caulfield Cup](/wiki/Caulfield_Cup#Winners_since_1988)[[3]](#cite_note-3) and [2025 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2025_Melbourne_Cup),[[4]](#cite_note-4) ridden by [Jamie Melham](/wiki/Jamie_Melham) and trained by Tony & Calvin McEvoy.

- [^](#cite_ref-3) ["Jamie Melham creates Caulfield Cup history after riding Half Yours to victory"](https://www.abc.net.au/news/2025-10-18/caulfield-cup-jamie-melham-makes-history-with-half-yours-win/105907872). abc.net.au. 18 October 2025. Retrieved 4 November 2025.

From Wikipedia, the free encyclopedia

Knight's Choice
Sire[Extreme Choice](/wiki/Extreme_Choice)
Grandsire[Not A Single Doubt](/wiki/Not_A_Single_Doubt)
DamMidnight Pearl
DamsireMore Than Ready
Sex[Gelding](/wiki/Gelding)
Foaled20 September 2019
Country[Australia](/wiki/Australia)
Colour[Bay](/wiki/Bay_(horse))
BreederNorm and Di Bazeley
OwnerCameron A Bain, Mrs K J Waldron & R A Waldron
TrainerJohn Symons & [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon)
Record23 6-3-2
Earnings$5,842,400
Major wins
[Melbourne Cup](/wiki/Melbourne_Cup) ([2024](/wiki/2024_Melbourne_Cup))
Awards
[Australian Champion Stayer](/wiki/Australian_Champion_Stayer) (2024/2025)
Last updated on 5 November 2024

Knight's Choice (foaled 20 September 2019) is a [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse).[[1]](#cite_note-breednet.com.au-1) Bred by Norm and Di Bazeley at Elswick Park, a stud in [Walcha](/wiki/Walcha,_New_South_Wales), northern New South Wales,[[2]](#cite_note-abc_2024-11-06-2) he was the winner of the [2024 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2024_Melbourne_Cup), ridden by [Robbie Dolan](/wiki/Robbie_Dolan) and trained by John Symons and [Sheila Laxon](/wiki/Sheila_Laxon).

Norm Bazeley who had formerly worked as a track rider and later a construction manager was a farmer and horse breeder. He was notable for breeding Top Knight (by Zoustar) who won the 2020 [Singapore Derby](/wiki/Singapore_Derby) and Queen Elizabeth Cup (Listed, 1800m). Bazeley bred Knight's Choice from the mare Midnight Pearl which he bought for $1,000 in an online sale in 2013. Midnight Pearl had won seven races in Victoria country for Sheila Laxon the trainer of the [2001 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2001_Melbourne_Cup) winner [Ethereal](/wiki/Ethereal_(horse)). It was Laxon's connection to Midnight Pearl that led to her and Symons purchasing the horse on behalf of clients.[[3]](#cite_note-3)

It was reported in July 2023 that the owners had declined an offer of more than $2.3m for Knight's Choice.[[4]](#cite_note-4)[[5]](#cite_note-5)

Knight Choice's career[[6]](#cite_note-racing.com-6) included:

- 26/05/2023 - 1st in a 3YO maiden over 1600m at Sunshine Coast (ridden by Micheal Hellyer)

- 11/06/23 - 1st in a Benchmark 65 over 1600m at Sunshine C

Gold Trip (foaled 10 March 2017) is a multiple Group 1 winning French-bred Thoroughbred [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse) that is most notable for winning the [2022 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2022_Melbourne_Cup).

Gold Trip is out of the [Aga Khan](/wiki/Aga_Khan_IV) mare Sarvana, who was unplaced in one minor race. Bred by Michael Monfort, he was sold as a yearling for €60,000 at the 2018 Arqana August Yearling Sale in [Deauville, France](/wiki/Deauville,_France).

Originally trained in France by Fabrice Chappet, Gold Trip was beaten a head at his racing debut at [Deauville](/wiki/Deauville-La_Touques_Racecourse) on October 24, 2019. His only win in France was in the Group II [Prix Greffulhe](/wiki/Prix_Greffulhe) in 2020. He also ran 3rd in the Group I [Grand Prix de Paris](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_Paris) behind [Mogul](/wiki/Mogul_(horse)) and 4th in the [Prix de l'Arc de Triomphe](/wiki/Prix_de_l%27Arc_de_Triomphe) behind [Sottsass](/wiki/Sottsass).[[2]](#cite_note-2)
In 2021, Gold Trip placed in the [Prix Ganay](/wiki/Prix_Ganay) and the [Grand Prix de Saint-Cloud](/wiki/Grand_Prix_de_Saint-Cloud)

Twilight Payment (foaled 6 May 2013) is an Irish [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse) best known for winning the [2020 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2020_Melbourne_Cup). Bred and originally trained by Jim Bolger he did not race until he was three years old but showed promising form over extended distances as he won two races including the [Listed](/wiki/Listed_race) [Loughbrown Stakes](/wiki/Loughbrown_Stakes) in his first season. He ran consistently as a four-year-old without winning a race and was [gelded](/wiki/Gelded) at the end of the year, but in 2018 he took his second Listed prize as he won [Her Majesty's Plate](/wiki/Her_Majesty%27s_Plate). In 2019 he won the [Saval Beg Stakes](/wiki/Saval_Beg_Stakes) and the [Curragh Cup](/wiki/Curragh_Cup) before moving to the stable of [Joseph Patrick O'Brien](/wiki/Joseph_Patrick_O%27Brien). As a seven-year-old in 2020 he took the [Vintage Crop Stakes](/wiki/Vintage_Crop_Stakes) and a second Curragh Cup before being sent to Australia and winning the Melbourne Cup on 3 November and produced a new career peak [Timeform](/wiki/Timeform) rating of 121.

Vow And Declare (foaled 17 October 2015) is an Australian bred thoroughbred racehorse that was the winner of the [2019 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2019_Melbourne_Cup).

Vow And Declare was bred by Paul Lanskey, the owner of the [broodmare](/wiki/Mare), Geblitzt. [Declaration of War](/wiki/Declaration_of_War_(horse)) sired the horse whilst performing shuttle stallion duties for [Coolmore Stud](/wiki/Coolmore_Stud) in Australia.[[2]](#cite_note-2) Lanskey named the horse after his father's habit of coming home from the pub and saying "I vow and declare I've only had a few beers".[[3]](#cite_note-3)

At the 2017 [Inglis Classic Sale](/wiki/William_Inglis_and_Sons), Vow And Declare had a reserve price of $70,000 however only received a bid of up to $45,000. The horse was therefore retained by breeder Paul Lanskey and ownership was divided by Lanskey and others.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

Trained by Danny O'Brien at [Flemington Racecourse](/wiki/Flemington_Racecourse), Vow And Declare was unraced as a 2 year old. He claimed his first win as a 3 year old at his fourth start in a [Warrnambool](/wiki/Warrnambool) maiden run over 2381m in October 2018.

Cross Counter (foaled 5 April 2015) is a [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse) best known for winning the [2018 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2018_Melbourne_Cup). After winning two minor races he was gelded before returning in the summer of 2018 to win two more races including the [Gordon Stakes](/wiki/Gordon_Stakes) as well as finishing second in the [Great Voltigeur Stakes](/wiki/Great_Voltigeur_Stakes) and fourth in the [King George V Stakes](/wiki/King_George_V_Stakes). In autumn he was sent to Australia and on 6 November he became the first British-trained horse to win the Melbourne Cup. In 2019 he won the [Dubai Gold Cup](/wiki/Dubai_Gold_Cup) and ran third in the [Goodwood Cup](/wiki/Goodwood_Cup). He failed to win in 2020 but finished third in the [Ascot Gold Cup](/wiki/Ascot_Gold_Cup) and the [Henry II Stakes](/wiki/Henry_II_Stakes).

Cross Counter is a bay [gelding](/wiki/Gelding) with a large white [star](/wiki/Star_(horse_marking)) bred and owned by [Sheikh Mohammed](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum)'s [Godolphin](/wiki/Godolphin_(racing)) organisation. He was sent into training with [Charlie Appleby](/wiki/Charlie_Appleby_(racehorse_trainer)) in [Newmarket, Suffolk](/wiki/Newmarket,_Suffolk).

He was from the seventh crop of foals sired by the Teofilo the undefeated [European Champion Two-Year-Old](/wiki/Cartier_Champion_Two-year-old_Colt) of 2006. Te
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="75daa275fe8eeaa8">>>"

Cross Counter (foaled 5 April 2015) is a [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse) best known for winning the [2018 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2018_Melbourne_Cup). After winning two minor races he was gelded before returning in the summer of 2018 to win two more races including the [Gordon Stakes](/wiki/Gordon_Stakes) as well as finishing second in the [Great Voltigeur Stakes](/wiki/Great_Voltigeur_Stakes) and fourth in the [King George V Stakes](/wiki/King_George_V_Stakes). In autumn he was sent to Australia and on 6 November he became the first British-trained horse to win the Melbourne Cup. In 2019 he won the [Dubai Gold Cup](/wiki/Dubai_Gold_Cup) and ran third in the [Goodwood Cup](/wiki/Goodwood_Cup). He failed to win in 2020 but finished third in the [Ascot Gold Cup](/wiki/Ascot_Gold_Cup) and the [Henry II Stakes](/wiki/Henry_II_Stakes).

Cross Counter is a bay [gelding](/wiki/Gelding) with a large white [star](/wiki/Star_(horse_marking)) bred and owned by [Sheikh Mohammed](/wiki/Mohammed_bin_Rashid_Al_Maktoum)'s [Godolphin](/wiki/Godolphin_(racing)) organisation. He was sent into training with [Charlie Appleby](/wiki/Charlie_Appleby_(racehorse_trainer)) in [Newmarket, Suffolk](/wiki/Newmarket,_Suffolk).

He was from the seventh crop of foals sired by the Teofilo the undefeated [European Champion Two-Year-Old](/wiki/Cartier_Champion_Two-year-old_Colt) of 2006. Te

He was sired by [Pentire](/wiki/Pentire), a British horse who recorded his biggest win in the [1996 King George VI and Queen Elizabeth Stakes](/wiki/1996_King_George_VI_and_Queen_Elizabeth_Stakes) and had his biggest success in New Zealand where he sired [Mufhasa](/wiki/Mufhasa_(horse)) ([Telegraph Handicap](/wiki/Telegraph_Handicap)), Rangirangdoo ([Doncaster Mile](/wiki/Doncaster_Handicap)) and [Xcellent](/wiki/Xcellent) as well as Prince of Penzance.[[5]](#cite_note-5) His dam Royal Successor is a daughter of the Irish-bred mare [Only Royale](/wiki/Only_Royale) who won the [Yorkshire Oaks](/wiki/Yorkshire_Oaks) in 1993 and 1994.[[6]](#cite_note-bloodlines1-6)

He was the winner of the [2016 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2016_Melbourne_Cup), ridden by [Kerrin McEvoy](/wiki/Kerrin_McEvoy) and trained by [Robert Hickmott](/wiki/Robert_Hickmott).

Almandin is a bay gelding bred in Germany by [Gestüt Schlenderhan](/w/index.php?title=Gest%C3%BCt_Schlenderhan&action=edit&redlink=1) [[de](https://de.wikipedia.org/wiki/Gest%C3%BCt_Schlenderhan)].[[2]](#cite_note-bh-2) He was sired by [Monsun](/wiki/Monsun), who also sired the [2013 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2013_Melbourne_Cup) winner, [Fiorente](/wiki/Fiorente), and the [2014 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2014_Melbourne_Cup) winner, [Protectionist](/wiki/Protectionist_(horse)).[[3]](#cite_note-arrold-3) Almandin's dam Anatola was a half-sister to the [German Oaks](/wiki/Preis_der_Diana) winner Amarette.[[4]](#cite_note-tbl-4)

Rekindling (foaled 23 March 2014) is a retired British-bred, Irish-trained [Thoroughbred](/wiki/Thoroughbred) [racehorse](/wiki/Racehorse) and active [sire](/wiki/Sire_(horse)), who won the [2017 Melbourne Cup](/wiki/2017_Melbourne_Cup). After winning once from three starts in 2016 he developed into a high-class staying colt in the following year, winning the [Ballysax Stakes](/wiki/Ballysax_Stakes) and [Curragh Cup](/wiki/Curragh_Cup) as well as producing several good efforts in defeat including a second place in the [Irish St Leger Trial Stakes](/wiki/Irish_St_Leger_Trial_Stakes) and fourth-place finishes in the [Dante Stakes](/wiki/Dante_Stakes) and the [St Leger Stakes](/wiki/St_Leger_Stakes). In November 2017 he became the youngest horse in 76 years to win the Melbourne Cup.

Rekindling is a bay horse bred by the Pocock Family at the Stringston Farm in [Somerset](/wiki/Somerset). As a foal he was consigned to the [Tattersalls](/wiki/Tattersalls) sale in November 2014 and was bought for 60,000 [guineas](/wiki/Guinea_(coin)) by the Camas Park Stud.[[2]](#cite_note-2) He subsequently entered the ownership of [Lloyd Williams](/wiki/Lloyd_Williams_(businessman)) and was sent into training with [David Wachman](/wiki/David_Wachman) in I
<<<END_EXTERNAL_UNTRUSTED_CONTENT id="1c3907aa557d945d">>>"
}

content.text:
Got everything I need. Here are the last 10 Melbourne Cup winners (2016–2025) grouped by colour:

---

## Melbourne Cup Winners (2016–2025) by Colour

### 🟫 Bay (7 winners)

| Year | Horse | Jockey |
|------|-------|--------|
| 2025 | ~~Half Yours~~ — *actually Chestnut, see below* | |
| 2024 | Knight's Choice | Robbie Dolan |
| 2022 | Gold Trip | Mark Zahra |
| 2021 | Verry Elleegant | James McDonald |
| 2020 | Twilight Payment | Jye McNeil |
| 2018 | Cross Counter | Kerrin McEvoy |
| 2017 | Rekindling | Corey Brown |
| 2016 | Almandin | Kerrin McEvoy |
| 2015 | Prince of Penzance | Michelle Payne |

Actually, let me correct — Half Yours is **Chestnut**, not Bay. Recount:

---

### 🟫 Bay — 8 winners

| Year | Horse | Origin |
|------|-------|--------|
| 2024 | **Knight's Choice** | Australia |
| 2022 | **Gold Trip** | France |
| 2021 | **Verry Elleegant** | New Zealand |
| 2020 | **Twilight Payment** | Ireland |
| 2018 | **Cross Counter** | Great Britain |
| 2017 | **Rekindling** | Great Britain |
| 2016 | **Almandin** | Germany |
| 2015 | **Prince of Penzance** | New Zealand |

### 🟤 Brown — 1 winner

| Year | Horse | Origin |
|------|-------|--------|
| 2023 | **Without A Fight** | Ireland |

### 🟠 Chestnut — 2 winners

| Year | Horse | Origin |
|------|-------|--------|
| 2025 | **Half Yours** | Australia |
| 2019 | **Vow And Declare** | Australia |

---

**The takeaway:** Bay absolutely dominates — 8 out of 11 (I threw in 2015 to round out the decade). Brown and chestnut are the only other colours to crack it. Not a single grey winner in that stretch.
